# How precision fermentation is pioneering sustainable food?

by Marco Marenchino,
Tuesday, 10th September 2024

![](https://www.materials-talks.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/shutterstock_384205462-1024x630.jpg)

In an era where sustainability is becoming a necessity rather than a choice, precision fermentation is an emerging technology in the [food industry](/en/industries/food-and-drinks). This cutting-edge technology not only promises to revolutionize how we produce food but also tackles some of the most pressing environmental challenges of our time. Here’s why precision fermentation is capturing the spotlight and how it’s setting the stage for a more sustainable future.

## What is Precision Fermentation?

Precision fermentation is a process where microbes — bacteria, yeast, or fungi — are engineered to produce functional ingredients by inserting DNA sequences that encode for specific proteins or molecules. These microorganisms can be used to create proteins for meat substitutes, dairy-free milk and cheese, or essential enzymes. Some foods have very complex structures which can be hard to engineer – for example, the texture of meat is defined by a complex network of muscle proteins. Precision fermentation is therefore applied to produce proteins with less overall structure, such as dairy proteins like casein which has a random coil shape, or minced meat where the texture is no longer intact.

### **Why Now?**

## Our precision fermentation solutions to typical challenges

## Malvern Panalytical: Your Partners in Discovery for Precision Fermentation

At Malvern Panalytical, we play a pivotal role in advancing precision fermentation. Our cutting-edge technology supports every phase of development, from optimizing processes to analyzing product stability and quality. With our solutions, you can assess particle size distributions, evaluate molecular structures, and ensure ingredient consistency, all while enhancing efficiency and maintaining high standards. With partners like Malvern Panalytical, the journey towards a sustainable and delicious future is well within reach.

# Protecting our food supply chain with precision fermentation

![](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/b7e7aaa5d1305bbeb504ef58df86cfb1?s=103&d=mm&r=g)

## Newsletter Signup - Under Article / In Page

"\*" indicates required fields

## Subscribe to our newsletter to get the latest biotech news!

**Many nations are experiencing a food security crisis caused by geopolitical conflicts, extreme weather and supply chain disruption. A wave of precision fermentation companies is working to protect from future food price shocks by producing sustainable protein ingredients from microbes rather than traditional farms.**

The world is facing a [protracted food security crisis](https://www.fao.org/newsroom/detail/global-report-on-food-crises-acute-food-insecurity-hits-new-highs/en) with the main drivers including economic turmoil from the COVID-19 pandemic, climate change and food price shocks resulting from the [war in Ukraine](https://www.labiotech.eu/trends-news/ukraine-war-europe-biotech/). Some of the poorest populations globally are the most vulnerable to the fluctuations in prices of wheat and other crops.

The crisis demonstrates humanity’s dependence on plant crops and animal farming for its food production, and how easily global disruption can jeopardize our food supply. However, an emerging form of food production based on cellular agriculture — farming animal cells or microorganisms — could allow society to more easily weather food security crises.

[Cultured meat](https://www.labiotech.eu/in-depth/cultured-meat-industry/) is one type of cellular agriculture entering the public eye. It involves growing animal muscle cells in a factory, which could be more sustainable and less resource-intensive than traditional animal farms.

Another hot area in cellular agriculture is the production of food through precision fermentation. Unlike traditional microbial fermentation, where microorganisms convert foods into beer, yoghurt and other well known products, [precision fermentation](https://gfi.org/science/the-science-of-fermentation/) involves turning microorganisms into mini-factories producing specific enzymes or protein ingredients. The raw materials needed to fuel precision fermentation range from sugars from waste biomass to greenhouse gases in the air, which could allow us to transition to a [circular, low-waste bioeconomy](https://www.labiotech.eu/in-depth/sustainability-circular-economy-bioeconomy/).

“Like everything precision fermentation has inputs and a supply, but the main inputs are electricity, which can be generated by renewables, and simple sugars, which have not shifted much in commodity pricing the past few months, and there is not really any supply chain disruptions in that area,” said Nick Cooney, Managing Partner at Lever VC, an investment firm focusing on companies in the alternative protein sector. “So relative to other ways of producing food, many of which have seen meaningful impacts and cost increases in recent months, precision fermentation is looking pretty good.”

Investments in fermentation players in the global alternative protein space tripled from almost $600 million in 2020 to [over $1.7 billion in 2021](https://gfi.org/blog/three-takeaways-from-a-record-setting-year-for-alternative-proteins/). This follows a [surge in consumer and investor interest](https://www.labiotech.eu/trends-news/solar-foods-meat-alternatives/) in meat alternatives that arose during the COVID-19 pandemic.

Now there are many companies forging a path to the market with gas and precision fermentation. Some products have already hit the shelves in the U.S., including Impossible Burger, which contains ingredients made using genetically modified yeast, as well as ClearEgg and EggWhite — egg protein ingredients produced from microbes by the EVERY Company.

## Suggested Articles

## [What’s the Secret to Making Returns in Industrial Biotech?](https://www.labiotech.eu/in-depth/industrial-biotech-investment-return/)

## [Base editing hits the clinic in the U.S.](https://www.labiotech.eu/in-depth/base-editing-enters-clinical-trials-us/)

## [The biggest biotech funding rounds in June 2025](https://www.labiotech.eu/in-depth/biotech-funding-rounds-june-2025/)

## [After decades of stagnation, is Japan’s biopharma industry set for an upswing?](https://www.labiotech.eu/in-depth/japan-biopharma-industry/)

## [How close are we to a cure for metastatic breast cancer?](https://www.labiotech.eu/in-depth/how-close-are-we-to-a-cure-for-metastatic-breast-cancer/)

Other startups are working their way towards the market. For example, the Israeli firm ImaginDairy recently [raised $28 million](https://www.labiotech.eu/trends-news/animal-free-milk/) to fund the development of dairy proteins produced via precision fermentation. And other companies like Solar Foods, Air Protein and LanzaTech are racing to harness microbes to turn waste gases like carbon dioxide into food for human consumption.

“Using carbon dioxide, hydrogen and other gases to feed microbes to generate proteins is so much more efficient and sustainable than conventional protein production,” explained Albrecht Wolfmeyer, head of the food startup-focused ProVeg Incubator.

“It needs less space, energy and water. Instead of adding to deforestation and climate change — around 80 percent of the destruction of our rain forests is caused by animal agriculture — it has the potential to recycle climate-wrecking gases. It is also clearly more ethical than raising and slaughtering billions of animals on an industrial scale.”

![food chain biotech](https://www.labiotech.eu/wp-content/uploads/2022/06/food-chain-biotech.jpg)
![food chain biotech](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns=%22http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%22%20viewBox=%220%200%20800%20533%22%3E%3C/svg%3E)

However, huge challenges remain for players producing protein via fermentation. One of the biggest is scaling up their technology to a point where the price of their sustainable protein can compete with traditional sources.

“From the companies we’ve spoken to and diligence in this area it does seem like it will be a fight to get the cost per kilogram of protein produced down to the same level as commodity plant-based proteins,” said Cooney.

The firm Deep Branch is one of many taking on the scaling challenge. The U.K. company uses microbes to turn carbon dioxide into sustainable protein for use in animal feed. Its approach differs slightly from precision fermentation as the microbes themselves are also an ingredient in the final protein product.

According to Deep Branch’s co-founder and Chief Technology Officer, Rob Mansfield, one of the main problems to solve is how to increase the volumes they use while keeping the gas-to-liquid transfer efficient. The company uses computational models and pilot experiments to predict the most efficient designs.

“This approach offers a powerful tool to assess the impact of all key parameters which might adversely affect the process within large-scale systems, such as gradients in temperature, pressure or pH,” explained Mansfield. He added that the company’s pilot plant will come online in the coming weeks.

Another big limiting factor is that there’s a finite amount of tank space in the world of fermentation. The main precision fermentation capacity is in the hands of multinationals including DuPont and Cargill, and their space is typically reserved for lucrative products including lactic acid and pharmaceuticals.

“There are a number of solutions to this, and we believe we’ll be seeing more added capacity to the tank space, including new facilities that will be built,” said Eyal Afergan, co-founder and CEO of ImaginDairy. “It will take time to completely resolve this bottleneck but looking at the global trend and where human nutrition is headed, we believe that working now toward the solution is the best thing to do.”

Sean Simpson, CSO and founder of LanzaTech, is also confident that the industry will adapt to growing demand for stability in food supplies. The firm uses its own facilities to generate 50,000 tons of ethanol and 3,000 tons of protein annually from gas emissions in a plant in China. “Many will be encouraged to take manufacturing much more into their own hands and so no longer rely on whole manufacturers,” he remarked.

Another question mark hangs over the fuel required to generate the electricity used in many precision fermentation plants. While renewable energy could provide the power required to fuel these processes, these forms of energy production aren’t fully reliable at present. Last year, for example, [the U.K. scrambled](https://fortune.com/2021/09/16/the-u-k-went-all-in-on-wind-power-never-imaging-it-would-one-day-stop-blowing/) to fire up gas and coal power stations when wind power generation fell sharply.

Nonetheless, future trends may unlock the potential of renewable energy, said Pasi Vainikka, CEO of the Finnish company Solar Foods. Solar Foods is developing a way to [convert carbon dioxide](https://www.labiotech.eu/startup-scout/solar-foods-space-mission-finland/) from the air into sustainable protein using just a vat of bacteria and electricity.

One notable trend that could change the game is the adoption of electric vehicles. They are creating demand for efficient battery storage whose benefits could ripple out to other industries, including precision fermentation. “The cars are not there yet, but if we think decades ahead, we are going through a transition,” said Vainikka.

![solar foods solein fermentation separator](https://www.labiotech.eu/wp-content/uploads/2022/06/solar-foods-solein-fermentation-separator.jpg)
![solar foods solein fermentation separator](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns=%22http://www.w3.org/2000/svg%22%20viewBox=%220%200%20768%201024%22%3E%3C/svg%3E)

While it could be feasible for protein made via gas fermentation to compete on price with animal proteins in the future, the jury’s still out whether it could go toe-to-toe with plant-based protein.

“At least with the current approaches, even if you leave aside the very large upfront [costs of setting up the facilities], the only way to get to price parity with key commodity plant proteins is if you’re able to get all of your energy from renewables and get some type of credits, subsidies or similar from using those renewables,” said Cooney. “There may well be a market for these sort of proteins, but personally I struggle to see how the benefits outweigh the [setup] and unit costs enough to represent a very big opportunity.”

However, precision and gas fermentation may not even need to reach price parity with current sources if the protein has a unique property or application. For example, the firm Protera uses precision fermentation to develop protein ingredients that can improve the shelf life of baked goods like bread.

“If we look at consumer behavior, people are today ready to pay more for better products (e.g. organic, locally produced), so there may not be an absolute need to get to price parity,” said Joško Bobanović, Partner, Industrial Biotech Strategy at the investment firm Sofinnova Partners. “In parallel, pressures on traditional animal protein producers to become more sustainable will continue to grow and additional costs will need to be incurred, consequently driving prices higher.”

In recent years, the media and general public have gained more awareness of how fragile the food supply chain can be. The recent food security crisis, combined with government initiatives such as the European Green Deal, is creating a perfect storm spurring the development of sustainable fermentation technology.

“It’s true that the raw materials used in precision fermentation could become more costly if the food security crisis gets more severe,” said Wolfmeyer. “Then again, the call for a more sustainable food system was never more urgent. Compared to conventional production and the current state, precision fermentation will turn out to be an inevitable part of the solution.”

July 14 2022: Sean Simpson’s job title corrected to CSO and founder of LanzaTech

FULL CONTENT:
# FAO.org

![](/fileadmin/templates/faoweb/images/FAO-logo-w.png)
![](https://www.fao.org/fileadmin/templates/faoweb/images/icons/home.png)

# Can precision fermentation offer a safe and sustainable future for food production?

![](fileadmin/user_upload/food-safety-quality/img/resources/5.png)

Precision fermentation is paving the way for a transformation of the food industry, offering a new method to produce food by harnessing the power of microorganisms. On 28 March 2025, the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) has released an insightful report on this rapidly evolving technology, with a focus on the related food safety considerations. The document entitled “[Precision fermentation – with a focus on food safety](https://doi.org/10.4060/cd4448en)” has been authored by Drs Sturme, van der Berg and Kleter from Wageningen Food Safety Research.

The report examines the intricacies of nomenclature, fundamental production principles, and the existing global regulatory frameworks surrounding these innovative food processes. An official launch webinar is planned, so keep an eye out for the announcement on the [relevant webpage](https://www.fao.org/food-safety/scientific-advice/crosscutting-and-emerging-issues/cell-based-food/).

Precision fermentation is a relatively new term that does not have an internationally agreed definition. It is often used to indicate specific fermentation processes, employed for the targeted and specific production of food compounds of interest, that are traditionally sourced from plants or animals. One tricky aspect that complicates nomenclature is not all precision fermentation processes necessarily employ novel techniques.

The fermentation processes are performed in a controlled environment, called bioreactors, which looks like big beer-brewing tanks, using microorganisms such as bacteria, yeasts, filamentous fungi or microalgae, as production hosts. The production process usually comprises of three phases: technology development, upstream fermentation, downstream processing.

To illustrate the practical applications, the newly published FAO report includes three detailed case studies showcasing different types of precision fermentation processes. It also summarizes the outcomes of two online regulatory consultations with more than 100 regulatory experts, where relevant information on the food safety frameworks of 35 different jurisdictions has been collected.

Rather than a simple overview, this publication can be used as a guide for food safety competent authorities worldwide to have compilations of good practices and lessons learned. By identifying common aspects of precision fermentation production processes and related hazards, as well as reviewing and comparing existing regulatory frameworks applicable to precision fermentation from various jurisdictions, it lays the possible groundwork for future regulatory actions. For countries hoping to understand the ways to ensure safety of food products derived from precision fermentation, this document can serve as a valuable reference point.

Curious to learn more about precision fermentation from a food safety angle? Look no further – the full publication can be downloaded at: <https://doi.org/10.4060/cd4448en>.

**For more information:**

--- SOURCE 5: Opportunities and challenges in precision fermentation - ScienceDirect ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929

SUMMARY:
The roundtable participants suggested that integrating PF into food production not only opens up new job opportunities in sectors like research and development (R&D), manufacturing and regulatory affairs but also drives demand for skilled workers. Navigating the complex regulatory and consumer landscape, the need for food safety evidence, alleviation of resource restrictions and addressing ethical concerns are major challenges identified by roundtable participants for the successful integration and acceptance of PF technologies in Australian food production. In Australia, the regulatory framework for PF food production and consumer market access is managed by two main governmental agencies, the Office of the Gene Technology Regulator (OGTR) and FSANZ. PF represents a transformative technology poised to impact the food industry in Australia by offering sustainable, innovative alternatives to conventional agricultural practices.

Advancing Australia’s food future: Opportunities and challenges in precision fermentation
=========================================================================================

Abstract
--------

Precision fermentation is transforming the food and beverage industries by leveraging microbial biotechnology to produce novel foods and ingredients. In Australia, the emerging precision fermentation ecosystem presents an opportunity to diversify and complement the food system, providing sustainable alternatives to conventional agricultural practices. However, these opportunities come with challenges related to scaling-up, regulation, safety, and consumer perception. This perspective paper, which uses insights gathered from a roundtable event that included participants from academia, industry and government, discusses the current landscape of precision fermentation in Australia, outlining opportunities, hurdles, and strategies to drive the growth and wider adoption of this technology.

Keywords
--------

Precision fermentation

Synthetic biology

Sustainable food production

Introduction
------------

Precision fermentation (PF) is a biotechnological process that uses genetically modified organisms to produce specific ingredients, such as proteins, fats or flavours, which are conventionally obtained from animal products or extracted from natural sources ([Graham and Ledesma-Amaro, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0001)). These ingredients can be used in a wide range of food products, complementing existing supply chains and help future proof the food system ([Cellular Agriculture Australia, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0002)). PF has emerged as a promising solution to address critical issues including resource depletion, environmental degradation and the escalating demand for food due to population growth ([Graham and Ledesma-Amaro, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0001)). To discuss the potential and challenges of PF foods, over 80 representatives from universities, industry and governmental agencies in Australia came together in 2024 to discuss the challenges and opportunities ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#fig0001)). The discussions, which are summarised in this perspective paper, have led to actionable recommendations, providing a roadmap for successful integration of PF technologies.

![Image 6: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666833525000929-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (474KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666833525000929-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (474KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666833525000929-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Opportunities and challenges of precision fermentation in Australia. Created with biorender.com.

### Current state of precision fermentation in Australia

PF is not a new concept in the food industry. Since the 1990s, chymosin, a key enzyme for milk coagulation, has been produced microbially and replaced the animal-derived rennet in most cheese making processes ([Emtage et al., 1983](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0003)). Several other food processing enzymes (e.g., amylase and lipase) along with minor food ingredients (e.g., food additives and nutritive substances) have been produced recombinantly ([Olempska-Beer et al., 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0004); [EFSA 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0005)). Recent increased investment and development in using PF to produce major food components (proteins, lipids and carbohydrates) has been partly driven by the urgent need to produce food more sustainably. While PF is gaining traction worldwide, its adoption in Australia remains limited, with few companies applying for regulatory approval. To produce macronutrients, PF has initially been used for developing alternative proteins (e.g., animal-free proteins). More recently, attention has also shifted towards producing lipids (fatty acids, phospholipids) and carbohydrates (oligosaccharides), as evident from patent filings in these areas ([Augustin et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0006)), indicating a growing interest in using PF to produce a broader range of food components.

Australia hosts a small yet innovative cluster of companies specialising in PF. At present, there are seven PF-based companies operating in Australia ([Cellular Agriculture Australia, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0002)). Eden Brew and Change Foods are known for their animal-free dairy products, while Nourish Ingredients focuses on producing animal-free fats. Companies like All G Foods, Eclipse, and Noumi are involved in the production of lactoferrin and Cauldron Ferm offer scaling up services for PF companies. While these companies are pioneering in their respective areas, the PF industry in Australia is still not operating at a large scale. The Australian government has recognised the potential of PF to food security, economic growth and environmental sustainability and has recently provided support for the necessary infrastructure for scaling up. Efforts are also being made to integrate this sector more thoroughly into Australia’s broader biotechnology and bioeconomy strategies, with recommendations for increasing resources to regulatory bodies like Food Standards Australia New Zealand (FSANZ) to better support the industry. The existing Food Standards Code and labelling requirements are considered adequate to manage new products produced through PF. To date, FSANZ has approved two PF-based food products: soy leghemoglobin produced by _Pichia pastoris_ for use in meat analogues and a series of human milk oligosaccharides (2′-O-fucosyllactose, lacto-N-tetraose, lacto-N-neotetraose, difucosyllatose, 6′-sialyllesactose sodium salt, 3′-sialyllaxtose sodium salt) produced by various strains of _Escherichia coli_ or _Corynebacterium glutamicum_ for use in infant formula ([FSANZ 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0007)).

### Opportunities

There was a general consensus among the roundtable participants that PF offers an opportunity for the food industry to enhance sustainability, diversify the supply chain, and meet evolving consumer demands.

**_Innovation in food industries_**. PF has catalysed several breakthroughs in the food industry, potentially transforming the future of food production. Ingredients typically found in complex mixtures can now be produced individually and recombined in novel ways to create new food products and diversify protein sources. For instance, Impossible Foods Inc uses PF to produce soy leghemoglobin, that can be added as an ingredient in plant-based burgers to more closely mimic the appearance and taste of meat-based burgers. This soy leghemoglobin can then be mixed with other non-PF components such as pea/soy proteins, plant-based fats, and methylcellulose, allowing the manufacturers to customise their final product while maintaining control over the quality and consistency, leading to innovative food solutions that appeal to a wide range of consumer preferences. PF also allows for the creation of personalised nutrition, tailored to cater specific energy or nutrient requirement of certain populations (e.g., phenylalanine-free formulas for infants with phenylketonuria), or assist in producing allergen-free foods (production of enzymes that specifically break down allergens). Additionally, PF can enhance nutrient content (e.g., omega-3 fatty acids), improve nutrient bioavailability (phytase-producing microbes), and extend shelf life (e.g., bacteriocin), contributing to increased food security and sustainability. Furthermore, PF offers a more sustainable and cost-effective way to produce ingredients that are typically rare, expensive or difficult to extract (e.g., vanillin). However, it is crucial to ensure that these PF products are economically accessible, to prevent disparities in food access.

**_Environmental benefits._** Australia continues to face significant obstacles in mitigating climate change and environmental impacts of food production. To meet its ambitious climate targets, Australia needs to decarbonize its overall greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions at an average annual rate of 17 Mt CO 2-e (metric used to compare the emissions of greenhouse gases based on their global warming potential relative to CO 2), >40 percent faster than the rate achieved since 2009 ([Australian Government Climate Change Authority 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0008)). Since agriculture represents about 16 % of Australia’s total GHG emissions, with dairy farming contributes roughly 19 % of these emissions ([Dairy Australia Ltd 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0009)), the implementation of PF could potentially offer substantial environmental benefits. Beyond reducing GHG emissions, PF provides a compelling opportunity to reduce fresh water usage and land requirements ([Järviö et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0010); [Godfray et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0011)). It also alleviates other pressures on planetary boundaries such as biogeochemical flows (e.g., reducing excessive use of fertilizers), ocean acidification and atmospheric aerosol loading ([Rockström et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0012)). However, a recent study indicated that beta-lactoglobulin produced via PF has comparable carbon and water scarcity footprints (5.5–17.6 t CO 2-e /t protein, 88–5030 m 3 world eq./t protein, respectively) to dairy-extracted milk protein ([Behm et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0013)). While PF demonstrates clear metrics for sustainability improvement at smaller scale, the benefits at larger scales become less certain due to the need for more accurate data. To address this, it is essential to incorporate sustainability assessments early in the development stage of PF products. Conducting comprehensive Life Cycle Analyses ensures that these products demonstrate a lower environmental footprint compared to natural food counterparts, verifying their sustainability as production scales up. Lastly by reducing the need for large scale farming, PF may contribute to the preservation of Australia’s unique biodiversity.

**_Economic benefits._** PF presents substantial economic benefits by fostering diversification and innovation within the economy. PF will facilitate the creation of innovative products that traditional methods cannot achieve, thereby opening new market opportunities and stimulating economic growth. Scaling-up PF-based manufacturing for animal protein production could be more cost-effective and efficient compared to conventional animal farming methods ([Good Food Institute 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0014)). Such innovations attract investments and generate high-value jobs, enhancing the economic well-being of regions that invest in this technology. A notable example is the government-supported initiative in the sugar industry, where ethanol is produced from sugarcane, leveraging the existing industry infrastructure in Queensland ([O'Hara, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0015)). Similarly, the successful deployment of PF in food production requires careful consideration of location, feedstock supply and government support to expedite development.

The roundtable participants suggested that integrating PF into food production not only opens up new job opportunities in sectors like research and development (R&D), manufacturing and regulatory affairs but also drives demand for skilled workers. This surge in demand has prompted educational institutions to collaborate with industry stakeholders in developing new curricula and training programs focused on essential skills required for PF. Additionally, PF creates an opportunity for universities to invest in advanced laboratory facilities, simulation tools and pilot-plant facilities to prepare students for real-world settings, developing cross-disciplinary courses and fostering collaborative research projects. Beyond technical skills, training on regulatory frameworks, health and ethical considerations is essential for navigating the approval processes for new PF products and addressing their societal and environmental impacts. Moreover, embedding students in industry environments through placements and internships is important to adequately prepare graduates for the demands of the PF sector.

In Australia, public-private partnerships are key to advancing PF initiatives. These partnerships often start with government grants that provide the initial capital needed for early-stage research. Industry partners then contribute additional funding and resources to further develop the project. For instance, the Australian government’s funding for the Food and Beverage Accelerator (FaBA) initiative, alongside contributions from industry stakeholders, has been instrumental in propelling the PF sector forward. As the industry matures, companies often seek venture capital to finance expansion into new markets or increase production capacity. Private equity firms usually step in at this stage, providing large-scale funding that enables significant business growth. This model is particularly effective for companies that have demonstrated the viability of their technologies and are ready to scale up operations. Additionally, diverse funding models such as venture capital, private equity and crowdfunding are promoted to effectively assist startups in scaling up.

### Challenges

Navigating the complex regulatory and consumer landscape, the need for food safety evidence, alleviation of resource restrictions and addressing ethical concerns are major challenges identified by roundtable participants for the successful integration and acceptance of PF technologies in Australian food production.

**_Complex regulatory landscape._** In Australia, the regulatory framework for PF food production and consumer market access is managed by two main governmental agencies, the Office of the Gene Technology Regulator (OGTR) and FSANZ. OGTR oversees the use of genetically modified organisms in production settings, while FSANZ is responsible for assessing and approving novel foods and ingredients, including those produced via PF. Under the current _Food Standards Code_ (Standard 1.5.1: Novel foods and Standard 1.5.2: Foods produced using gene technology), foods produced by PF must undergo a pre-market assessment as a genetically modified (GM) food and/or a novel food ([Graham and Ledesma-Amaro, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0001)). Industries are required to apply for an amendment to the _Food Standards Code_ for each new precision-fermented ingredient or food, unless it has already been reviewed in a previous application. The regulatory landscape is further complicated by the fragmented roles played across federal and state level governments. This fragmentation often causes regulatory bodies to adhere rigidly to established procedures, which can hinder the integration of innovative technologies like PF into the market. For the PF industry to thrive in a rapidly evolving market landscape, regulatory agility is essential yet challenging to achieve due to these complexities. A robust regulatory framework is not only vital for streamlining market entry but also for setting realistic expectations of PF’s capabilities. In recognition of the advances in GM food production, FSANZ is currently reviewing definitions for gene technology and new breeding techniques. Furthermore, there is broad consensus on the need to appoint an Australian Minister for Food to simplify regulatory processes, foster innovation and actively promote PF across the broader food industry. This recommendation aligns closely with both the 2023 Australian Parliament’s Agriculture Committee report and submissions to the 2024 Government Inquiry into the Future of Food and Beverage Manufacturing ([Parliament of Australia, 2025](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib26), [Parliament of Australia 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0022)).

**_Consumer acceptance and safety._** Consumer perceptions are critical for the success of PF technologies. Factors influencing consumer perceptions include the role of retailers, ingredient origins, labelling practices, global sustainability trends, taste and cost ([Brea et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0016)). Understanding these factors is vital to address consumer concerns and enhance acceptance. Misconceptions about GM foods and perceptions about naturalness of PF can pose significant barriers to acceptance ([Teng et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0017)). Clear and accurate labelling that distinguishes between GM organisms and products derived from GM processes is essential for building consumer trust. The choice of terminology used, such as "precision fermentation" or "cellular agriculture", needs to be carefully chosen to avoid confusion and mitigate negative sentiment. Studies have shown that consumers often have a limited understanding of food processing technologies, which can lead to scepticism and resistance towards new foods ([Lusk et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0018)). To mitigate this, it is recommended to improve public education and awareness by implementing targeted programs that explain PF technology, address potential misconceptions and emphasize its benefits.

The safety of food ingredients derived from PF is paramount and hinges on comprehensive regulatory assessments, environmental risk management, and strict controls over the purity and safety of both starting materials and final products. FSANZ currently employs a risk-based public health and safety assessment process for novel food ingredient applications ([FSANZ 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0019)). As PF technologies evolve, shifting from producing food processing aids to major food ingredients, this regulatory approach may need to be adjusted to effectively address the novel types and applications of these ingredients. Standardised testing and monitoring are required to maintain these safety standards. This may include long-term exposure studies, the development of advanced bioinformatics methods like machine learning for allergenicity prediction, stability monitoring of production hosts, and contaminant testing in growth media and final products ([Ong et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0020)). Additionally, addressing the safety concerns associated with using low-cost by-products as starting materials—which can introduce toxins, heavy metals, and allergens—is critical. Employing established risk analysis techniques, such as those built on Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Point (HACCP), is essential for preventing contamination and ensuring the safety of the final products. CRISPR (Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats) technology has revolutionised genetic engineering by allowing precise genome edits without incorporating foreign DNA. This technique could lower the risks associated with conventional plasmid-based genetic engineering such as horizontal gene transfer or the unintentional spread of antibiotic resistance genes to native microbes. Despite these advancements, it is crucial to conduct thorough safety evaluations to uncover any unintended genetic consequences, such as off-target effects, ensuring the technology's safe application.

**_Resource restrictions_**_._ The real impact of PF in food production will only be realised when it can produce foods at costs comparable to conventional systems. This demands efficient scaling, a reliable feedstock supply chain and regulatory support to achieve price parity with traditional food production methods. PF companies should have access to affordable accredited pilot scale manufacturing facilities. This could be done either by constructing new facilities or by upgrading existing ones. Currently, the lack of contract manufacturing organisations (CMOs) in Australia means the industry needs to rely on overseas CMOs, which can increase costs and complicate logistics and regulatory compliance. Repurposing underutilised facilities, such as decommissioned ethanol and dairy processing plants, for PF could mitigate this issue while also minimising capital expenditures. Scaling up PF can increase the demand for feedstocks such as sugar, corn and potatoes, leading to competition for resources. To mitigate the risk of monoculture, exploring alternative feedstocks such as agricultural byproducts or non-food crops would be beneficial. Recent work led by Queensland University of Technology involves establishing a CMO facility co-located in a sugarcane-growing region adjacent to an operating sugar factory, thereby providing easy access to sugar, biomass, and sugarcane co-products. Although PF is often highlighted for its efficiency and lower resource use compared to conventional agriculture, it still requires significant amount of water for processes like cooling, cleaning and for the fermentation itself. Fermentation processes also need controlled temperatures, resulting in high energy consumption and associated electricity costs. It is thus recommended to integrate green technologies such as renewable electricity or hydrogen production, into new PF facilities ([Lips, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0021)). The fast-paced environment of PF startups is also not adequately supported by the existing workforce. The final report to the Senate Inquiry on Food Security in Australia highlighted that the limited availability of skilled graduates in regional areas, where large-scale PF facilities or pilot plants are often established, could hinder the progress of the PF industry ([Parliament of Australia 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0022)). While theoretical knowledge of bioprocessing and synthetic biology is generally adequate, Australian industry stakeholders have identified practical experience in scaled-up operations as a critical need.

**_Ethics._** The ethical landscape surrounding PF is multifaceted, encompassing issues related to biosafety and biosecurity, ecological impact, intellectual property, and social justice. Central to these concerns are the environmental risks like ecological disruption and gene contamination, which are compounded by scientific uncertainties. Risk evaluations often focus narrowly on acute human safety risks and are influenced by commercial pressure, frequently overlooking broader ecological and societal impacts. Adopting new approaches for risk assessment ([Symons et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0023)), developing mechanisms to consolidate risk assessments across jurisdictions and stakeholders, and addressing pressures to expedite assessments are crucial for advancing PF responsibly. PF is often promoted as a solution to global challenges, which can set potentially unrealistic expectations that may overshadow alternative solutions and jeopardise long-term investment and public acceptance if the benefits are not delivered ([van Lente, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0024)). Intellectual property rights play an important role to play in fostering innovation but can also lead to corporate consolidation and power ([Moss, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666833525000929#bib0025)), impeding diverse innovation and favourable public interest outcomes. Additionally, the regulatory and technological processes that predominantly focus on scientific perspectives often overlook the intrinsic concerns of oppositional groups, leading to an underrepresentation of broader societal implications. A comprehensive ethical framework should incorporate transparency regarding both the potential benefits and the associated risks of PF technologies, thereby promoting the responsible production of PF-based foods.

Conclusion
----------

PF represents a transformative technology poised to impact the food industry in Australia by offering sustainable, innovative alternatives to conventional agricultural practices. Despite its potential for reducing environmental impacts and enhancing food security, the successful adoption of PF hinges on addressing regulatory, economic, and consumer acceptance challenges. Strengthening regulatory frameworks, promoting public-private partnerships, and fostering consumer trust through transparent lab

enriched_points.expanded_content: Precision fermentation provides a sustainable approach to mitigate food security crises driven by geopolitical conflicts, climate change, and supply chain disruptions, ensuring stable food supplies.

enriched_points.point_title: Potential to Reduce Reliance on Animal Agriculture
enriched_points.expanded_content: By leveraging microbial processes to produce protein and other ingredients, precision fermentation decreases the need for traditional animal farming, reducing environmental impacts and ethical concerns.

enriched_points.point_title: Contribution to a Sustainable Food System
enriched_points.expanded_content: This innovative technology supports a circular economy by utilizing waste biomass and greenhouse gases, promoting resource efficiency and minimizing ecological footprints in food production.
